evidence_quotes.quote: predatory digital contracts pertaining to subscription services
Videogames with online features that may shut down in the future, either blocking access to those online features, or the entire game (including singleplayer / "offline" features)
Software including a hidden system that forces a paid update on the user after a hidden time not listed directly on the storefront
stop companies from using terms like "ownership" when a product is expected to cease functioning after the sale

paragraphs.text: One of the examples given in the call for evidence for this initiative is predatory digital contracts pertaining to subscription services, but in recent times it has become more an more unclear when buying digital software whether or not you are buying a license to use the software / the software having an unspecified expiry date at the time of purchase, or buying the software itself. Examples of this include; - "Lifetime" access to software that would normally be sold under license, but these "lifetime" programs being a sort of license in and of themselves, and being dependant on DRM servers that may shut down in the future. - Videogames with online features that may shut down in the future, either blocking access to those online features, or the entire game (including singleplayer / "offline" features). - Software including a hidden system that forces a paid update on the user after a hidden time not listed directly on the storefront, or after a major software revision. I would appreciate it greatly if the initiative could be adjusted so that these predatory business tactics fall within the legal bounds of unfair digital contracts, and / or make it so that companies are required to disclose these practices on the customer before they buy the software / stop companies from using terms like "ownership" when a product is expected to cease functioning after the sale. It might also be prudent to require companies to have an end-of-life plan for these types of software, given they hinge on digital systems that only that company has, but can be replicated by consumers (whether they be individuals, or organisations), given correct instructions. My studies as an electrical engineer often require closed-source, paid software with extensive license checking and online content servers that could be shut down at any time, and given that my livelyhood will depend on these systems in the future, I would like to thank the commission for allowing citizens like me to give feedback on these regulations.
